data.title: Westrock Coffee Company Offers Potential, But It's Too Early For An Upgrade
data.description:
Back in September of 2024, one company that I decided to upgrade was none other than Westrock Coffee Company (WEST). This business sells coffee, tea, flavors, extracts, and ingredients solutions to its customers. It has other operations as well, such as those centered around selling commodity contracts related to forward sales of green coffee and also packaging its products on both a branded basis and a private label basis for resale. Even though this might seem like an easy type of business to capture upside from considering how popular these products are, I had previously been bearish about the firm because of its overall financial condition.

But because the future for the company was starting to look better, I ended up upgrading it from a ‘sell’ to a ‘hold’. Admittedly, at that time, I acknowledged that it was still a more speculative prospect. The company was far from an easy win. And since then, the market has disagreed with my assessment, pushing shares down 6.4% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 6.2%. However, in my mind, the fact that the stock is now cheaper, and that management continues to have a bright outlook for the company, indicates that there could be something here at some point in time. I do still think it is premature to upgrade it yet again. But if we can see management deliver on their promises, an upgrade might be possible at some point in time.

Another taste of Westrock Coffee Company

At this time, the most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Westrock Coffee Company covers the third quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. In fact, we don't even yet know when results will come out for the final quarter of last year. For the most recent quarter, however, revenue for the company came in at $220.9 million. This represents only a small increase over the $219.6 million the business reported one year earlier. All of this growth, and more, came from its Sustainable Sourcing & Traceability segment. This is the part of the company that focuses on selling commodity contracts regarding green coffee. Year over year, this segment saw revenue jump 32.8% from $42.8 million to $56.9 million. This was because of higher sales volume amounting to 35.9%.

As great as it was to see an increase here, the Beverage Solutions segment, which actually involves the sale of value added beverage solutions like coffee, tea, flavors, extracts, and ingredients, all of which are packaged and sold to customers, dropped from $176.8 million to $164 million. This decline of 7.2% was because of a $16.4 million plunge in coffee and tea product sales. Management claimed that difficult macroeconomic conditions had a negative impact on pod sales and other types of coffee sales. To be clear, Westrock Coffee Company has not been alone in experiencing this pain.

As an example, for the most recent quarter for which data is available, which would be the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year, Starbucks (SBUX) reported a 4% decline in global comparable store sales. This included weak sales throughout North America, which is its home market. This was driven entirely by an 8% plunge in the number of transactions. For the first nine months of its 2024 fiscal year, Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) reported a 2.6% drop in its US Coffee segment. For the third quarter on its own, the sales decline was 3.6%. As one other source pointed out, there are multiple issues facing the coffee industry. This includes higher costs and the impact of inflation on consumer budgets.

Even though revenue increased, profitability for the company did worsen. Net income went from $12.8 million to negative $14.2 million. This was because of a jump in selling, general, and administrative costs from 16.9% of sales to 20.9%. Management attributed this to an $8.9 million increase in costs associated with the startup of the firm's extract and ready to drink facility located in Arkansas. Unfortunately, other profitability metrics followed suit. The one exception was operating cash flow, which went from negative $39.4 million to negative $0.3 million. However, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a worsening from negative $2.3 million to negative $5.5 million. Over the same window of time, EBITDA for the company fell from $11.6 million to $10.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2024 compared to the same time in 2023. Because of challenges in the coffee and tea product space, mostly stemming from lower demand for roast and ground coffee, as well as single serve cups, revenue dropped year over year. Meanwhile, profitability was mixed. Net income and adjusted operating cash flow both worsened. However, operating cash flow and EBITDA improved on a year-over-year basis.

Clearly, this is a name that continues to face some pressures. However, the future does look fairly bright. For starters, even though Westrock Coffee Company is a fairly small player with a market capitalization as of this rating of $601.9 million, it has a large footprint. Over 8,000 retail establishments serve the products that it sells. When it comes to convenience store locations, we get another 20,000 or more. The company counts three of the top five largest retailers in the US and 15 of the top 20 quick service restaurants in the nation as its customers. It also sells its products to six of the top ten food distributors. All combined, the company services over 100,000 customer locations.

But it's not just about the past. It's also about the future. For instance, even though coffee sales have been weak as of late, management still maintains that there is a consumer shift in the direction of cold coffee beverages. Personally, I can attest to that. I don't like warm coffee. But I do love a nice French vanilla iced coffee. Meanwhile, the firm is also working to expand its Beverage Solutions business to parts of Europe and the Asia Pacific. Earlier in this article, I also mentioned what the business is working on in Arkansas. Specifically, back in the second quarter of 2024, the firm launched a 570,000 square foot manufacturing facility focused on extraction and creating ready to drink products. This followed, just a single quarter earlier, the launch of its 530,000 square foot distribution facility in that same area.

These investments should, if everything goes according to plan, boost overall volume potential for the company. Management described a scenario where they see a rapid scaling up of ready to drink can volume starting in the first quarter of 2025. That should help EBITDA to come in for the 2025 fiscal year at between $80 million and $100 million. To put this in perspective, for 2024, the company had been projecting EBITDA of $50 million. However, that's with $10 million of scaling up expenses factored in. So on an adjusted basis, I would peg this number at $60 million.

Furthermore, we are looking at about $10 million to $15 million of additional scaling up expenses for 2025. So at the midpoint, on an adjusted basis, EBITDA for this year should be around $102.5 million. Given that profitability has been negative, the only thing to really strip out of this to get a good proxy for adjusted operating cash flow would be interest expense. Based on my estimates, that should be around $28.8 million annually. This implies adjusted operating cash flow for 2024 of $31.2 million and for 2025 of $73.7 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. On a forward basis is specially, shares look somewhere between slightly undervalued and fairly valued. However, relative to other food and drink oriented businesses, the stock is currently a bit on the pricey side. In the table below, you can see how it stacks up against five similar firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, three of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. This number ticks up to four of the five on an EV to EBITDA basis.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Westrock Coffee Company 19.3 21.5
Adecoagro S.A. (AGRO) 3.0 3.4
SunOpta (STKL) 41.8 22.8
B&G Foods (BGS) 3.3 11.7
Mission Produce (AVO) 9.2 8.5
Vital Farms (VITL) 21.0 18.3

Obviously, as I detailed earlier in this article, there is some uncertainty in this market for now. But in the long run, I suspect that the industry will be just fine. Management estimates that the global coffee and tea market is worth about $438 billion. And over the next few years, they expected to grow at a rate of about 4% per annum. This is focused solely on the wholesale side of things. When it comes to the ready to drink category of coffee, management is particularly interested in the $22 billion opportunity that they claim exists there. This ties in nicely with cold coffee, which seems to be particularly favored by younger generations.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that Westrock Coffee Company is a fairly interesting company. However, I don't think that I'm ready to pull the trigger on an upgrade at this time. There's still a lot of banking on the future. And as anybody who has been an investor for a long time can tell you, the future can be incredibly uncertain. In the event that management can start delivering on its projected financial results, my mindset on the matter might change. But for now, I think maintaining the company as a ‘hold’ candidate makes the most sense.

data.title: AVGX: This Leveraged Play On Broadcom Just Isn't Hacking It, Here's Why
data.description:
Over the past year, there has been an increased level of interest in single-stock ETFs that use leverage to deliver returns in multiples of the underlying's return. Among the newer ones to feature is the Defiance Daily Target 2X Long AVGO ETF (NASDAQ:AVGX) that was incepted in August 2024. The 1.29% ER might be a deal breaker for many ETF investors, but my analysis here shows me that there are more serious issues with this $94 million fund.

More About AVGX

This equity ETF uses leverage via swap agreements on the daily price return of a single underlying asset: stocks of Broadcom (AVGO). As of February 20, only about 9.4% of the fund's assets are invested directly into the underlying. The rest of the 212.25% is made up of various swaps that essentially take long positions in expectations of significant price action, and some government obligations.

This price action is then amplified through the swaps, and that's essentially the main goal of this fund: to deliver a daily return on AVGO that's equal to 2x the price gain of the underlying.

This kind of leverage is extremely risky even when executed by qualified and experienced professionals because the performance is overly dependent on an asset that shows relatively steady price appreciation. For that reason, the SEC continually keeps warning retail investors to 'look before they leap' into such exotic ETFs. Of course, the exotic has now become commonplace, and this is just one of dozens of such exchange-traded products that promises one of two things: exciting mid-double-digit percentage yields OR sizzling alpha over and above the underlying asset's returns.

Remember, though, that this type of ETF is a trading tool, not an investment in and of itself. That's because of various factors that can deplete your assets down to zero if you're not watchful and just do a buy and hold on it. I've covered this topic extensively in my earlier articles on leveraged ETFs, so I invite you to read those if you're interested.

The Inner Workings of AVGX

Equity swaps are essentially agreements to trade a cash flow from one equity asset for a periodic pre-agreed rate of interest that's typically pegged to SOFR, and based on the notional value of the swap. The point here is that the party that's bullish on a stock typically agrees to pay that pre-agreed amount, and in exchange, the counterparty pays the first party the actual gains of the underlying. The first party obviously expects the difference between the pre-agreed amount and the actual gains to be positive, and considerably so, but if that delta is negative, the first party not only pays the pre-agreed amount but also compensates the counterparty for the negative return.

In any case, what's relevant to a product like this is that these swaps should only be entered into if the sentiment around that asset is bullish; if you're bearish or simply want to get rid of some risk because the asset is too volatile for your taste, you'd better be on the other side of the swap, receiving the pre-agreed amount. The benefit to the counterparty, among other things, is that they get to keep the voting rights on the stocks they own; they're simply trading away potential upside in return for a more or less predictable income stream and lower risk.

AVGX Performance: Real-life Is Very Different!

The leverage used by AVGX is intended to return twice the gains of AVGO on a daily basis. We've already seen that, and it sounds like a simple enough arrangement. The problem with this strategy is that with very volatile stocks, the returns can range from tremendous to abysmal to everything in between. Sudden shockers can wipe out millions in asset value in a single trading day, but the gamble is that it can generate returns in multiples of those of the underlying. So the strategy is essentially high-risk-high-reward.

Let's move ahead to the ETF's actual performance. We saw that it only invests less than 10% in AVGO stock, which means any major price movements to the upside are effectively not captured by the stock in the portfolio but largely by the swaps. On the flip side, that gives it some protection via the downside not being compounded by both the loss on the swaps as well as price depreciation when AVGO declines on any given trading day.

Let's use the price action on the last trading day before this article (Friday, February 21) as an example:

First, the market itself declined by 1.6%, and because AVGO has a high beta (24M beta of 2.28 per SA's AVGO ticker home page), it moved down by a steeper 2.46%. Second, see how AVGX lost more than double that at 5.36%? That's the leverage working against the ETF. If the fund held a higher percentage of AVGO, that drop would have been even greater.

The problem is, come Monday, February 24, everything resets to zero; in other words, there's no clawing back from the drop. A 5.36% drop is NOT neutralized by a 5.36% gain on the next trading day. In order to recoup that loss, the ETF needs to rise by a higher percentage.

An easy-to-follow analogy is losing 5% on a $100 investment. At the end of day one, you're left with $95, but gaining 5% the next day only brings you back to ($95 + 5% = $99.75). That phenomenon is known by many terms - volatility drag, time decay, beta slippage, etc., and it can completely wipe out all your capital.

The added problem here is that the sum total of those down days can drag on that 2x targeted return over time, as I'll explain later on. Now, I'll present the fund's total return performance since its inception.

It's clear that the swaps aren't really working as intended. Theoretically, the fund should have delivered a total return of around 80%, or roughly double that of AVGO, but it has failed to do that.

There's an explanation for that, if you'll bear with me. I'll now show you how the ETF fared before and after AVGO saw that bump in its price based on the Q4 report released on December 12.

Inception to December 12

The graph above tells me a few things. First, that the fund doesn't perform to its target when AVGO is moving sideways, which it was before Q4 results came out on December 12 post-market. From August 2024 until then, the stock only gained 11.65%. The problem is, due to that volatility decay, AVGX delivered the exact same total return as AVGO.

Second, if the down days should exceed the up days, this fund's return will be in trouble. In other words, this is not a fund you want to hold when AVGO is on the decline - or even if the stock inches upward in a very volatile manner.

December 13 to Date

Next, let's look at AVGX's performance from the morning after that Q4 announcement, when management reported a 220% revenue growth rate in AI revenues.

What's pretty clear to me is that AVGX only achieves its superior daily return when there are clear moves to the upside, but when the underlying stock is range-bound, it's more of a hit or miss; when the stock declines, it's far worse.

Closing Thoughts

To be fair to the fund managers, they've delivered a better total return than AVGO - about 9% to 10% above what you might have gained just holding AVGO.

If you think that's a good deal, that's your choice, obviously. However, I see the risk as being greater than the reward here. Keep in mind that the fund only performed on the sudden spike and during daily upswings. Moreover, the actual TR isn't anywhere close to a 2x return. Add to that the fact that AVGO is trading at precarious valuations in the neighborhood of 17 times forward sales and 34 times forward cash flow, and you'll see risks from every direction. All for a 10% alpha over the underlying? Is it really worth the trouble?

As such, I wouldn't recommend AVGX to leverage AVGO until the latter shows a much clearer and more sustained uptrend. I don't see that happening for AVGO at the present time, but that could quickly change.

And when that uptrend does get triggered by a more sustainable catalyst like continued triple-digit percentage growth in AI revenues in the next quarter, positive news on its reported deal with Apple to produce in-house AI server chips, or its top line gradually reflecting the "AI revenue Serviceable Addressable Market, or SAM, for XPUs and network in the range of $60 billion to $90 billion in fiscal 2027 alone", that would be the time to engage using AVGX. Not right now. Hold.

data.title: Comcast: A Slow-Moving Company In A Rapidly Changing Industry
data.description:
Change is not always good for more traditional companies. While some corporations are able to adapt to a more difficult operating environment, industries often change faster than management teams can adapt.

One of the fast-changing sectors of the economy right now is the cable and wireless industries. With TV users increasingly shopping around and wireless customers usually not loyal to any one brand, a once static industry has become much more competitive.

A company that has consistently struggled to adapt to the changing competitive landscape is the Comcast Corporation (NASDAQ:CMCSA). The cable company had 22 million cable subscribers just 9 years ago, today Comcast has barely over 12.5 million cable subscribers. The industry leader was a large company in a fairly uncompetitive industry just a decade ago, but today Comcast is ill-equipped to compete in the fast-moving industries the cable company operates across.

Comcast has offered investors total returns of negative 9.78 percent over the last 5 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) is up nearly 96 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about Comcast in June 2023, and I rated the company a sell. I am updating my coverage of the company today to a strong sell. The cable and wireless company is seeing continued losses in the video and cable industry, and increased competition in broadband. Management has also not been able to successfully monetize wireless subscriber growth to reverse longer standing negative issues with the core business, including more margin compression. The cable company's park revenues are steady, but also not growing significantly either.

The cable and wireless company's recent fourth quarter again highlighted many of the deeper issues the company continues to struggle with. Comcast reported EPS normalized actual of $.96 a share, beating consensus analyst estimates by $.10 a share. The company also stated revenues actual were nearly $31.92 billion, slightly beating consensus estimates by nearly $292 million. The most important metrics for Comcast are subscriber numbers and subscriber growth, these indicators are more important than short-term earnings per share reports. Even though the company beat slightly on the top and bottom line, shares sold off hard on this report because of Comcast's disappointing broadband subscriber numbers in the quarter. The cable and wireless company lost 139,000 customers in the quarter, which was much higher than consensus estimates for losses of 91,000 customers. Comcast also reported that theme park revenues in the fourth quarter fell 3.9 percent on a year-to-year basis to $838 million.

Comcast's cable and video business is structurally broken, and the company has been losing customers for nearly a decade from this service.

The cable company lost nearly 2 million cable subscribers in 2022 alone, and Comcast lost 311,000 video users in 2024 after dropping 389,000 video users in 2023. The fact that video and cable users are using the company's services less each year hurts both revenues and margins, since a falling customer base also limits the industry leader's ability to market more profitable products and services from other parts of the business to people as well.

The most concerning part of Comcast's fourth-quarter earnings report was the disappointing broadband subscriber numbers. There has been increasingly fierce competition in this part of the business, and the company's management team has once again been slow to adapt in the fast-paced and changing industry. The affordable connectivity program which offered subsidies to lower-income families switching using broadband services ended on June 1st, people are working less remotely, and there is also increased competition from companies such as T-Mobile, who added more broadband users last quarter than Comcast despite having a more limited network. The fact T-Mobile is having more success than the Comcast in broadband despite having less capacity in the company's network shows the cable and wireless company is having issues with marketing as well. Comcast's reviews have shown that many people do not have a positive view of the cable and wireless company.

While Comcast did report an increase in the number of wireless subscribers by nearly 5 percent, the growth and revenue from this division is not coming close to offsetting wide structural issues in other core businesses of the cable and wireless company.

Comcast added 307,000 mobile subscribers in the quarter, bringing the total number of wireless subscribers the company has 7.8 million. The company offers the service through a deal with Verizon.

Comcast's growth in the mobile field isn't offsetting the revenue decline and margin compression the cable provider is seeing from losses in other parts of the cable and wireless company's core business.

Comcast's revenues have grown by less than 3 percent since 2022, and the cable and wireless provider's operating margins are at the lower end of the 15-year range, at 18.83%.

This is why Comcast also looks overvalued. The cable and wireless company currently trades at 9.54x expected forward GAAP earnings estimates, but analysts are expecting Comcast to grow revenues at barely 1 percent over the next 5 years.

Analysts are also expecting Comcast's earnings per share growth to be just 5-6% over the next 4 years, and those estimates are misleading because of the significant buybacks management continues to authorize, that have mostly added debt to the company's balance sheet. The cable and wireless provider authorized a new $15 billion dollar share buyback plan last quarter. Comcast now has nearly $94 billion in long-term debt, which is close to the company's record high of $107 billion.

Comcast looked very well positioned just 10 years ago, when the company had nearly 22 million cable subscribers in what once was an industry growing. Today, this traditional company is facing increased competition across the cable company's core businesses, and management has not shown the ability to adopt or develop a viable plan to offset the structural issues Comcast hasn't been able to deal with. While the company is investing in new technologies such as DOCSIS 4.0 and the company has seen some subscriber growth with the new Peacock service, this new streaming product is still not profitable either, suggesting promotions and heavy discounting are needed just to maintain subscribers. Comcast's struggles in broadband suggest new investments in technology won't be sufficient to repair the core business, either. Indeed, even if Comcast is able to stabilize the failing cable business, the company's broadband and wireless businesses face increasingly tough competition as well.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial: Downgrading To Hold And Maintaining A Rosy Earnings Outlook
data.description:
The stock price of Pinnacle Financial Partners, Inc. (NASDAQ:PNFP) has rallied by 19% since I last gave a buy call on the stock in October 2024. I’m now expecting earnings to surge this year on the back of high-single-digit loan growth and some margin expansion. Further, the absence of losses on sales of securities will lead to year-over-year earnings growth. I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $7.62, in 2025, which is close to my previous earnings estimate. I’m also expecting a total expected return of negative 6.6% after the recent price rally. Hence, I’m downgrading Pinnacle Financial to a hold rating.

Efficiency Ratio May Finally Stabilize

Since my last report on Pinnacle Financial released in October 2024, the company has released its earnings for the third and fourth quarters of the year. The actual earnings for 2024 were 4.8% below my previous earnings estimate. The biggest reason for the earnings miss was that the operating expenses were greater than expected.

In recent years, Pinnacle Financial has been focused on expansion and new recruitment, which is a good plan to grow the business in my opinion. However, the execution of that plan hasn’t been good, which is visible in the worsening of the efficiency ratio (calculated as non-interest expenses divided by total revenue). The efficiency ratio has worsened in each of the last three years, as shown in the chart below. (I've adjusted the efficiency ratio for one-time losses on sales of securities). The operating expenses have risen sharply, but the revenue growth has not risen in proportion.

The management mentioned in the conference call that it made a new record in 2024 for hiring revenue producers. It can take some time for new recruits to build up the loan pipeline, so I still have hope that the efficiency ratio will no longer continue to worsen.

However, there is risk because of the tariffs and their impact on inflation. I think there’s a chance that inflation may be higher in 2025 compared to 2024. This will create pressure on Pinnacle Financial to raise its salaries.

Overall, I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to stand at 56.6% in 2025, which is close to the adjusted ratio of 57.2% in 2024.

Expansion and Hiring to Sustain Loan Growth

Pinnacle Financial’s loan portfolio’s growth picked up pace in the second half of the year, leading to full-year growth of 8.5%. I’m optimistic about loan growth this year because Pinnacle Financial hired a “record number” of revenue producers in 2024, as mentioned in the conference call. I’m not sure about the exact number of employees hired because the 10-K filing hasn’t yet been released.

Further, the company expanded its network in Jacksonville, FL, Atlanta, GA, Washington D.C., Alabama, and Kentucky during 2024. This strategic expansion cost $2.6 billion, according to details given in the earnings presentation. I’m expecting this expansion to bear fruit in 2025. Pinnacle Financial’s network distribution now stands as follows.

As the company’s loans are concentrated in Tennessee and North Carolina, the economies of these two states are instrumental in driving Pinnacle Financial’s loan growth. As shown below, the unemployment rate of Tennessee has increased since my last report in October, while North Carolina’s rate has changed very little. Of note, the current unemployment rates of both states are still below the national average.

Considering the management’s measures and the regional economies, I’m expecting the loan book to grow by 8.2% in 2025, which is slightly below last year’s growth rate. Further, I’m expecting the company to successfully raise enough deposits to fund its loans; therefore, I’m expecting deposits to grow in tandem with loans.

Upcoming Fixed-Rate Loan Maturity to Lift the Margin

The net interest margin grew by 8 basis points in the third quarter and then remained unchanged in the final quarter of 2024, which was largely in line with my expectations. For 2025, I’m not expecting any further Fed funds rate cuts. Nevertheless, the net interest margin can continue to grow this year because Pinnacle Financial has some very low-yielding fixed-rate loans that are scheduled to mature in 2025. This will give the company an opportunity to renew these outgoing loans at much higher rates. The management is expecting the renewed loans to carry rates of around 6.5% to 7.0%.

According to my calculations, given below, the maturity of the fixed-rate loans can raise the net interest margin by 15 – 20 basis points.

Avg yield on outgoing loans a 4.9%
Target renewal rate - upper limit b 7.0%
Target renewal rate - lower limit c 6.5%
Expected difference in yield - upper limit d=b-a 205 bps
Expected difference in yield -lower limit f=c-a 155 bps
Outgoing loans as a percentage of total loans g 9.8%
Impact on margin - upper limit h=g x d 20 bps
Impact on margin - lower limit i=g x f 15 bps

The yield curve’s movement could also help the margin, as explained by the management in the conference call through the following quote:

Our fixed rate loans have a negative beta, which obviously helps slow the pace of these loan rate decreases. This is where the yield curve is critical. If it steepens, those fixed rate renewals become even more helpful.”

Unfortunately, the yield curve has NOT steepened so far this year. After the yield curve’s inversion ended in late 2024, the yield curve hasn’t moved much. As shown in the chart below, the latest yield curve is almost the same as the yield curve at the end of last year (see the blue and orange lines).

I think it's best to be cautious in the current environment of heightened uncertainty. Therefore, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by only 8 basis points in 2025.

Expecting the EPS to Bounce Back up to $7.62

Pinnacle Financial’s non-interest income fell last year because of a one-time loss on sales of securities reported in the second quarter of the year. Further, the income from the Bankers Healthcare Group (“BHG”) declined last year. (Side note: BHG originates loans for medical professionals and patients and sells these loans for gain.) For 2025, I’m expecting the income from BHG to be stable and the rest of the non-interest income to revert to the mean in the absence of losses on sales of securities.

The normalization of non-interest income and the growth of loans and net interest margin will likely drive earnings this year. The expected stable efficiency ratio will lead to higher non-interest expenses, which will hold back earnings. Further, I’m expecting the provision expense for loan losses to depend on loan additions.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $7.62 per share for 2025, up 28% year-over-year. My new earnings estimate is close to my previous estimate of $7.70 given in my last report on the company. I haven’t made any big changes to any income statement line item.

Downgrading to Hold

After raising its dividend per share by 9% in January to $0.24, Pinnacle Financial is now offering a dividend yield of 0.8%.

To update my valuation, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book value (“P/TB”) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my EPS forecast for 2025 gives a target price of $99.1.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my TBVPS forecast for 2025 gives a target price of $112.3.

The average price target for the end of 2025 comes out to 105.7, which is 7.4% below the February 21 closing market price of $114.21.

In my last report, I gave a December 2025 target price of $104.5 and adopted a buy rating. Since then, the stock price has rallied by around 19%. My new target price and the last closing market price suggest a price downside of 7.4%. Adding this downside to the dividend yield gives a total expected return of negative 6.6%. This total expected return isn’t bad enough for a sell rating. Hence, I’m downgrading Pinnacle Financial to a hold rating.

data.title: Wingstop: Time To Feast Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I last wrote about Wingstop (NASDAQ:WING), where I rated the stock a “hold,” despite the company reporting strong Q2 results and the management raising forward guidance. My main concern behind Wingstop during my previous two ratings had been its valuation, which I believed was priced to perfection, leaving little room for execution error.

However, since then, the stock has declined over 45% from its peak in September, as the company saw its revenue growth rate decelerate as a result of tougher comps. Particularly in Q4 FY24, the company saw its total revenue grow 27.4% YoY to $161.8M, with domestic same-store sales growing at 10.1%, compared to 21.2% in the previous year.

While revenue and Adjusted EBITDA growth is expected to slow in FY25, the management remains confident in achieving its long-term targets of $3M in AUV (Average Unit Volume) while it expands to 10,000 locations globally. The main drivers behind the management’s confidence in its long-term targets stem from their continuous investments in digital transformation, brand awareness campaigns, and menu innovations, while digital sales as a percentage of system-wide sales grow higher sequentially.

However, the stock has also received a slew of earnings per share and revenue downgrades over the past month, which has dampened both investor confidence and the forward price-to-earnings multiple. Yet, looking at the big picture, I believe we have finally entered a moment where the risk-reward looks extremely attractive with a potential upside of 70% over a 3-year investment horizon.

Assessing both the “good” and the “bad,” I will upgrade my rating to a “buy” with a price target of $396.

Despite revenue growth slowing, management is confident on its $3M AUV target.

Wingstop reported its Q4 FY24 earnings, where total revenue grew 27.4% YoY to $161.8M, missing analyst estimates. For the full year FY24, Wingstop opened a total of 349 net new franchise locations, bringing their total system-wide restaurant count to 2563, up 15.7% YoY, with domestic franchises accounting for over 84% of all system-wide restaurants.

Despite domestic same-store sales growing at a decelerating pace of 10.1% YoY for the full year FY24 (down from 21.2% in the previous year), Wingstop managed to grow its AUV by 17% YoY to $2.3M, driven primarily by transaction growth while they continued to invest in digital transformation, menu innovation, and brand awareness.

While the management expects domestic same-store sales to decelerate further in FY25 in the low to mid-single digits, the management is confident on achieving its long-term targets of $3M in AUVs while scaling their global unit count to 10,000 through a combination of proprietary and brand awareness strategies.

“There remains a significant runway ahead of Wingstop, and we believe we are entering this next phase of growth. We've spent a fair amount of time extracting insights from our digital guests, learning from our consumers as well as those that are more broadly frequent restaurants. What we're learning gives us confidence in the strategies we're executing, and we continue to have significant opportunities to gain our fair share in the restaurant industry.”

Particularly, the management discussed that they will be expanding their presence in the NFL, WWE, UFC, and NBA to grow their brand presence, while their AI-enabled kitchen operating platform should lead to a reduction in quote times and enhance the speed of service, thus unlocking pent-up consumer demand and improving team member productivity in the restaurants.

The management also shared that over 10% of their total restaurants are already generating AUVs north of $3M from superior unit economics generated from a high level of predictability into food costs. This in turn is leading to strong demand from brand partners, with a robust pipeline of nearly 2000 restaurant commitments under development agreements at the end of 2024. Plus, Wingstop is still in the nascent stages of its international expansion plans, which gives it plenty of whitespace ahead to march towards its $3M AUV target.

Growing digital sales is another reason for optimism.

Another contributing factor behind the management's long-term targets is tied to its growing mix of digital sales, which climbed to 70% of system-wide sales in Q4, up from 69% in the previous quarter. In 2024, the company launched its proprietary tech stack, MyWingstop, with their digital database consisting of 50M customers, up 30% YoY.

During the earnings call, the management discussed that they have seen an increase in the number of guests opting for notifications and registering on the platform over the past two quarters, which is translating to higher purchase frequency as well as improved ROI (return on investment) on their personalization strategies. Moving forward, the management is optimistic that they will be able to further fine-tune their database so that they can deliver a more meaningful and engaging experience for their new and existing customers, which should lead to higher volume and purchase frequency, thus enabling the company to meet its long-term AUV target.

Strong unit economics, although the growth rate in Adjusted EBITDA is expected to slow

Shifting gears to profitability, Wingstop saw its cost of sales as a percentage of company-owned restaurant sales increase by 250 basis points on a year-over-year basis as a result of higher food and packaging costs. In the meantime, the company generated $56.3M in Adjusted EBITDA, up over 43% YoY, with a margin expansion of over 400 basis points to 34.8%, as they continued to anchor their SG&A expenses at 2.5% of system-wide sales, driven by their highly franchised, asset-light model.

For FY25, the management expects to grow its Adjusted EBITDA by 15% YoY, which should translate to $243.8M. Assuming revenue grows as per consensus estimates in the high teens range from a combination of low- to mid-single digit domestic sales growth, along with a global unit growth rate of 14-15%, Adjusted EBITDA margins should remain at FY24 levels or see a slight deceleration of no more than 100 basis points on a year-over-year basis.

Revisiting my valuation: 70% Potential Upside ahead

Looking forward, I will take the forward consensus estimates for revenue growth in the high teens, which should translate to over $1B in revenue by FY27, especially as it makes progress towards its $3M AUV target while opening 10,000 total locations globally. At the same time, its digital sales should play a pivotal role in helping the company achieve its long-term targets as it drives highly personalized offers to its growing members, resulting in higher volume and purchase frequency.

In terms of profitability, I will take management’s guidance of Adjusted EBITDA growing 15% YoY in FY25, which translates to an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 33%. Assuming it is able to anchor its operating margins in the 33-35% range from its superior unit economics and supply chain strategies, it should generate close to $350M in Adjusted EBITDA by FY27, which is equivalent to a present value of $299M, when discounted at 8%.

Assigning a price-to-earnings multiple of 38, which is roughly 2.2 times higher than the average price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500, given the growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $396, which is roughly 70% higher than its current levels.

I will note however that Wingstop may seem overvalued when compared to the median forward price-to-earnings multiple of its sector, which stands at 16.46, compared to Wingstop’s forward price-to-earnings multiple of 65. However, it is important to note that Wingstop is projected to grow its earnings at a much faster rate than its peers that include McDonald's (MCD), Chipotle (CMG), Domino's Pizza (DPZ), Wendy’s (WEN), Shake Shack (SHAK) and therefore, justifies a superior price-to-earnings premium in my opinion. Plus, over the last six months, the stock has seen its forward price-to-earnings also shrink by over 40%, which I believe has created an attractive long-term investment opportunity for investors

Risks to Thesis

Since September 2024, Wingstop stock has seen its share price decline over 45%. I believe part of the reason is tied to the company facing tougher comps as it sees its growth rates across both its top and bottom lines decelerate in pace, while macroeconomic pressures persist.

At the same time, the stock has also received a slew of earnings per share and revenue downgrades over the past month, which has contributed to the shrinking of its forward price-to-earnings multiple.

However, what is important to note is that its earnings per share are expected to accelerate in FY26 as the comps environment normalizes, while revenue growth is expected to remain in the high teens range as it progresses towards its $3M AUV target along with 10,000 locations globally.

My final verdict and conclusions

In my previous posts on Wingstop, I had repeatedly warned of its stretched valuation as a cause for concern, despite its impressive growth story amid a tough macroeconomic environment, along with superior unit economics.

Since the time of my last publication, the stock price has declined over 45%, and I believe we have finally arrived at a moment where the risk-reward looks extremely attractive to initiate a long-term position at these levels.

What is important to note is that despite the company facing tougher comps in FY25, the management is confident in achieving its long-term growth targets of $3M in AUV while it expands to 10,000 locations globally. I believe it should be possible as the company invests in its digital transformation strategies along with driving brand awareness campaigns and menu innovation that is translating into strong demand among its brand partners, as well as robust user acquisition and adoption, particularly on its MyWingstop platform.

Therefore, I upgrade my rating to a “buy” with a price target of $396, representing an upside of 70%.

data.title: Margin Balances Suggests Risks Are Building
data.description:
New Coronavirus Discovery Shakes Markets

Last week, we discussed that continued bullish exuberance and high levels of complacency can quickly turn into volatility. As we noted then, introducing an unexpected, exogenous event can soon lead to a price decline if investors begin to reprice forward expectations. On Friday, that unexpected event presented itself when China, and most notoriously, the Wuhan Lab, reported the discovery of a new coronavirus in bats. To wit:

“Another coronavirus feared to be powerful enough to spread through humans has been discovered in China. In scenes eerily reminiscent of the beginnings of Covid, researchers at the infamous Wuhan Institute of Virology detected the new strain living within bats.

HKU5-CoV-2 is strikingly similar to the pandemic virus, sparking fears that history could repeat itself just two years after the worst was declared over. The new virus is even closer related to MERS, a deadlier type of coronavirus that kills up to a third of people it infects. Virologist Shi Zhengli, known as ‘Batwoman’ for her work on coronaviruses, led the discovery, published in a top scientific journal.”

While this may or may not be an issue, it doesn’t take much to cause a market reversal when markets are elevated and highly bullish. As discussed last week, the market broke out of the bullish consolidation and set new record highs. However, that breakout was brief, and the news on Friday led to a retest of the 50-DMA and the triggering of the MACD “sell signal.” Such indeed suggests there could be more price pressure next week.

However, we want to give the market a few days to review the news. First, Friday was also an “options expiration” event, which added to the selling pressure in the market when the “virus” news hit. Secondly, the virus news will be thoroughly digested over the weekend and, by Monday, should be discounted in the markets. However, if the virus begins to replicate and spread, markets will have to evaluate further the risk to economic growth and earnings.

For now, we suspect markets will likely stabilize between the 50 and 100 DMA, reducing the bullish exuberance in the market. This will provide a better base on which to build as we enter the last three months of the seasonally strong period of the year. As noted previously, while there are certainly market risks, like sharp increases in leverage and speculation, there are currently no signs of that breaking—at least not yet.

Friday’s sell-off is precisely why we continue reiterating the need to rebalance risk and manage allocations.

Speaking of leverage, I want to expand on the margin debt discussion from Tuesday.

What Are Margin Balances And Why Are They Important?

On Tuesday, I touched on the continued increase in investor exuberance in the market. One sign of that exuberance is increases in margin balances. However, many may be unaware of what margin balances are and why they are noteworthy.

“Margin debt refers to the money an investor borrows from a brokerage firm to buy securities, using their existing portfolio as collateral. It allows investors to leverage their capital, meaning they can control more assets with a smaller upfront investment. While margin can amplify gains, it also increases risks, as losses are also magnified when markets decline.” – ChatGPT

When investors buy stocks on margin, they must deposit an initial amount (known as the initial margin), typically 50% of the purchase price per Regulation T set by the Federal Reserve. The brokerage firm lends the remaining balance.

For example, an investor with a $30,000 investment account invested in Apple (AAPL) can borrow up to $15,000 on margin to buy additional equities. However, using margin balances carries several risks.

  1. Margin Call – If the value of the securities drops below a certain level (the maintenance margin, usually 25%), the brokerage may issue a margin call, requiring the investor to deposit more funds or sell securities to cover losses.
  2. Amplified Losses – If an investor buys a stock on margin and it declines, they could lose more than their initial investment.
  3. Market Impact – High levels of margin debt in the market can contribute to bubbles and crashes. When markets decline sharply, forced liquidations from margin calls can lead to accelerated selling and increased volatility.

Margin balances represent the amount of speculation occurring in the market. In other words, margin debt is the “gasoline,” which drives markets higher as the leverage provides for the additional purchasing power of assets. However, leverage also works in reverse, as it supplies the accelerant for more significant declines as lenders “force” the sale of assets to cover credit lines without regard to the borrower’s position. As noted on Tuesday, it should be unsurprising that there is a high correlation between investor sentiment and margin balances.

The last sentence is the most important. The issue with margin debt is that the unwinding of leverage is not at the investor’s discretion. That process is at the discretion of the broker-dealers that extended that leverage in the first place. (In other words, if you don’t sell to cover, the broker-dealer will do it for you.) When lenders fear they may not recoup their credit lines, they force the borrower to put in more cash or sell assets to cover the debt. The problem is that “margin calls” generally happen simultaneously, as falling asset prices impact all lenders simultaneously.

Margin debt is not an issue – until it is.

So, where are we currently?

Margin Debt Confirms The Exuberance

I want to start with what I wrote in that previous post and then expand further into margin balances and the potential warning signal for investors. Let’s start where I left off.

“When we specifically look at margin debt, a loan against underlying collateral in brokerage accounts, those debt levels have surged to a record. As shown above, the year-over-year rate of change in debt is rising sharply but certainly can go further if retail exuberance continues.”

“However, look at the red line, “free cash balances.” As noted, margin debt supports the advance when markets rise as investors can leverage additional leverage to increase buying power. Therefore, the recent rise in margin debt is unsurprising as investor exuberance climbs. The chart below shows the relationship between cash balances and the market. I have inverted free cash balances, so the relationship between increases in margin debt and the market is better represented. (Free cash balances are the difference between margin balances less cash and credit balances in margin accounts.).“

“Note that during the 1987 correction, the 2015-2016 “Brexit/Taper Tantrum,” the 2018 “Rate Hike Mistake,” and the “COVID Dip,” the market never broke its uptrend, and cash balances never turned positive. Both a break of the rising bullish trend and positive free cash balances were the 2000 and 2008 bear market hallmarks. With negative cash balances at another all-time high, the next downturn could be another “correction.” However, if, or when, the long-term bullish trend is broken, the unwinding of margin debt will add “fuel to the fire.”

As noted, rising leverage is the “fuel” for bull market advances. What makes rising margin debt levels more dangerous is when retail exuberance feeds into all risk assets simultaneously.

While the increase in margin balances is alarming, it is even more concerning when we remember that retail investors are also piling into leverage ETFs and options (another form of leverage) at a frightening pace. As I posted on X this past week, retail investor allocations to ETFs now exceed levels seen in 2021.

In other words, the amount of leverage in the system today is far more significant than represented solely by margin balances. Nonetheless, that increase in risk-taking has fundamentally supported stock prices in recent months.

However, when discussing margin balances, I often get questions about the “rate of the increase.”

A Need For Speed

The “rate of increase” is a crucial factor concerning margin balances. If the market is rising, and margin balances are slowly increasing over time, such does not provide a good indication of “investor greed.” Margin balances should be expected to increase over time as the market grows. However, if the rate of increase is sharp, that is a stronger indication that investors are becoming exuberant, which is typical of late-stage market advances. One measure we track is the rate of change from the margin balance’s lowest point over the last 12 months. Unsurprisingly, margin balances have risen 30% from those lows. While current levels of increase are not as high as previous ones, the increase does show the rise in investor “greed” in recent months.

We can also apply technical analysis to margin balances to study the rate of change in debt levels.

Margin data goes back to 1959, giving us a lot of history to study its relationship to the market. The chart below is a “stochastic indicator” of margin debt overlaid against the S&P 500. (The stochastic indicator is a momentum indicator developed by George C. Lane in the 1950s. The analysis examines the most recent margin debt level relative to its previous high-low range. In other words, the indicator measures the momentum of margin debt by comparing the closing level with the range over the past 21 months.)

The stochastic indicator represents the speed and momentum of margin debt level changes. This means the stochastic indicator changes direction before the market, making it a leading indicator. The stochastic indicator at 100 (its maximum level) has typically preceded a market reversal and represents the sharp increase in investor risk-taking.

We see the same when we apply a Relative Strength Index to margin balances. The relative strength index (RSI) is a momentum oscillator measuring the velocity and magnitude of changes to margin balances. Readings above 80 have typically preceded short to intermediate-term corrections or consolidations. With a current reading above 90, the increase in margin balances since October 2022 is notable.

As noted, it isn’t just investors’ “sentiment” that is exuberant. Yes, investors are very confident that stock prices will be higher. Crucially, not only are they confident, but they are aggressively allocating money toward risk and doing it with leverage.

However, that increase in leverage is essential to pushing asset prices higher.

It is also a warning.

Watching For The Warning Sign

I want to make a crucial point here. Margin debt, like valuations, is a “terrible market timing” indicator and should not be used as such.

Rising levels of margin debt measure investor confidence. Investors are more willing to take out debt against investments when shares are rising, and they have more value in their portfolios against which they can borrow. However, as noted above, the risk is when falling asset prices reduce the amount of credit available. The subsequent liquidation of assets must occur to bring the account back into balance.

I agree and disagree that margin debt levels are simply a function of market activity and have no bearing on the market outcome.

In March 2020, the double-whammy of collapsing oil prices and economic shutdown in response to the coronavirus triggered a sharp sell-off fueled by margin liquidation. Most investors have forgotten about 2020 or, worse, assume it can’t happen again for various short-sighted reasons. However, the “gas tank” is once again full as investors are more exuberant now than at the market’s peak in 2021.

One warning sign that has been a good indicator to reduce portfolio risk is when the margin balances fall below the 12-month moving average. Margin balances are well above that level, keeping portfolios allocated toward equity risk. However, previously, when that warning signal was triggered, markets spent time either consolidating or correcting.

Sure, this time could indeed be different. That has remained investors’ “siren song” throughout history. However, as Sentiment Trader summed up the last time we wrote on this topic, such is usually untrue.

“Whenever some of this data fails to lead to the expected outcome for a few weeks or more, we hear the usual chorus of opinions about why it doesn’t work anymore. This has been consistent for 20 years, like…

  • Decimalization will destroy all breadth figures (2000)
  • The terror attacks will permanently alter investors’ time preferences (2001)
  • The pricking of the internet bubble will forever change option skews (2002)
  • Easy money will render sentiment indicators useless (2007)
  • The financial crisis means relying on any historical precedents are invalid (2008)
  • The Fed’s interventions mean any indicators are no longer useful (2010 – present)

All of these sound good, and for a time it seemed like they were accurate. Then markets would revert and the arguments would get swept into the dustbins of history.”

We are not suggesting that margin balances are warning of an imminent crash. However, they indicate that investors are very exuberant about future market returns and have taken on increased risk. More notably, they are doing so this time by leveraging investments with additional leverage.

When the market does reverse, the devastation to many investors will likely be much more immense than most assume.

How We Are Trading It

While we remain long-biased in our equity portfolios, we have begun to reduce some of our big winners (take profits) and continue to add to our more defensive-oriented positions. While we certainly want to participate in the market’s current upside, we will also give up some gains to protect against the eventual reversion.

Although it certainly “feels” like the market “just won’t go down,” it is worth remembering Warren Buffett’s sage words.

“The market is a lot like sex, it feels best at the end.”

In the short term, holding higher cash levels will indeed provide some drag between our portfolio and the major market index. However, if an event hits the markets, our preparation should protect us against the initial “bite.”

We remain “bullish” on the markets, as momentum is still in play. However, we are taking precautionary actions for the “just in case” scenario.

To spin a bit of Warren’s quote:

“If you engage in the market in an unprotected fashion, you may not want the unexpected surprise.”

Given the market uncertainty, the high levels of complacency, and the risks to stability, managing portfolio risks is worth considering. That is why we have started rebalancing portfolio risk accordingly. With both technical and sentiment readings suggesting the short-term market risks are elevated, taking some “small” actions now is wise, which you will likely appreciate later.

  1. Tighten up stop-loss levels to current support levels for each position.
  2. Hedge portfolios against more significant market declines.
  3. Take profits in positions that have been big winners.
  4. Sell laggards and losers.
  5. Raise cash and rebalance portfolios to target weightings.

Therefore, from a portfolio management perspective, we have to trade the market we have rather than the one we think should be. This can make battling emotions difficult from week to week. However, as noted, we expect a correction sooner rather than later, providing a better risk/reward opportunity to increase equity exposure if needed.

How To Read This Table

One of the early warning indicators the risk-range report provides is the moving average crossover (last column) of intermediate and long-term measures. A few months ago, only bonds and international traded on bearish crossovers. Since then, we have sharply increased the number of bearish crossovers in various sectors and markets. Such suggests an ongoing deterioration in price action in the markets, which has yet to spill over into the S&P 500 completely. While this does not mean that investors should immediately become bearish, it does suggest that risks are building for a more substantial market correction at some point if these sub-markets do not start to improve soon.
